chunk_summary: Micronutrient deficiency mechanisms in pediatrics involve low intake, increased requirements, and excess loss, requiring phenotype-first testing and age-aligned prophylaxis strategies to prevent missed diagnoses and double dosing.
learning_objectives: Understand the three primary mechanisms of micronutrient deficiency: low intake, increased requirements, and excess loss.
Learn how to apply phenotype-first testing strategies using high-yield markers like ferritin and bone profiles.
Recognize the importance of age-banded and program-aligned prophylaxis to prevent omission or double dosing.
Identify co-occurring deficiency syndromes and avoid single-nutrient diagnostic approaches.
key_facts: Low bioavailability diets, such as cereal-phytate-heavy diets, reduce absorption of iron and zinc.
Rapid growth phases (infancy, adolescence) and recovery from illness increase micronutrient requirements beyond typical dietary supply.
Chronic diarrhea, malabsorption conditions (e.g., celiac disease, IBD), and parasitic infections impair absorption or increase nutrient loss.
Ferritin testing should account for inflammation to improve diagnostic accuracy.
Prophylaxis strategies must align with age and national programs to avoid double dosing from therapeutic foods or tonics.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Ferritin concentrations for the assessment of iron status should consider inflammation markers to improve diagnostic accuracy. (Source: WHO ELENA, Reference 2)
document_key_topics: Mechanisms of micronutrient deficiency in pediatrics
Phenotype-first testing strategies for micronutrient deficiencies
